title: Federico Varese: Mafias on the Move
abstract: According to one contemporary myth we live in a globalized age, where distance is dead and, as the title of a bestseller some years ago declared (Friedman, 2005), the world is flat. There is ample evidence that this is not so true. Distance is alive and kicking—it matters in international trade (Berthelon and Freund, 2008) and in many other human endeavours, including the production of immaterial goods such as patentable ideas (Picci, 2010). There is ample evidence showing us that, rather than flat, our world is rather bumpy (Leamer, 2007). According to a corollary to the myth of flatness, organized crime is also globalized, and its perpetrators, taking full advantage of contemporary transportation and communication technologies, choose the location of their undertakings just as a top manager would ponder where to delocalize production to maximize profits. Books such as Sterling (1995) and Glenny (2008) contributed to form this perception at the academic level, and much media coverage of various types of ‘foreign’ mafias freely roaming around the world adds nails in the coffin of a more nuanced view.
